item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't actually see any similarities between the two phenomenon besides the involvement of clothing, though. One is substitutiary locomotion caused by psychometric imprinting of a person's soul on their garments, and the other is a detergent that makes you clean yourself to death when you wear clothing that's washed with it. What sort of crosslinks did you have in mind?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, WikiWalk team!

Just wanted to call attention to some low-hanging fruit in cross-linking this with SCP-325.

If the researchers didn't make that connection in-universe, they should be fired.

IRL, I'd say the two skips take the same basic effect in radically different directions. So there's space enough for both on the list.
Congratulations! Love this - as others have said, it's quirky and understated and just depicts a world we don't really know the meaning of. Wonderful, in the strictest sense of the word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the perfect, sublime weirdness that permeates the Foundation. Not everything has to be a great, grand battle, some of it just has to be viscerally disconcerting and horrifying in a very strange and uncomfortable way. Well done, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tentative upvote. Will change to downvote if the Foundation does not compensate employees for lost clothing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the article before the note, and the note only made me upvote harder.
"Just burn your clothes for us so they don't phantomime you at the work place. Okay, thanks."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's never quite clear, but do the manifestations use the person's actual clothing? As in, you wouldn't find them in the person's wardrobe while a manifestation is happening?

+1 to simple, neat skips making a comeback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first impression was another SCP-325, but the tone kind of took away from that. But it was the HR letter that sold it. That a group that handles some of the weirdest stuff imaginable can still make a bureaucratic and impersonal HR letter like that.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt the same way. I like things that give an insight to the day to day operations of the Foundation under the… unique conditions it has to deal with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey man, all caps is pretty unnecessary, please cut that out.

On topic: It is pretty neat, just not up my alley. Also, good to see you back PeppersGhost. Missed you <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We ain't afraid of no…anomalous floating clothing!

Welp, maybe a little. Burn it with fire anyway. Thanks!

…upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great as it is, although I was suspecting some manner of involvement of that pitch-black looking man in the background…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I upvoted this for the final letter. Not because the skip itself is unique or has anything overly interesting, but because you captured the tone of a routine HR letter PERFECTLY. I thought it was a really great insight into the sort of HR letters that are just common and routine for the Foundation.

Like most workplace memos will say things like, "Please wash your break room dishes after use." Or "this is a friendly reminder that personal cell phone use is not allowed on company time." The Foundation asks you to kindly remember to burn your clothes every six months so they don't animate into a specter. Classic!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad to see you back!

with experience in psychometric anomalous is

Typo?

Overall, I'm kinda neutral on this one. I always enjoy short and simple weirdness, and this is well written, but this isn't really doing much for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ You know what I think?
- Other than suggesting you catch up on all the stuff I've written...

I'm imagining that this was just a common anomaly involving people who wore certain sets of clothes often, leaving behind a trace of themselves. Foundation starts exposing itself to the anomaly, it starts focusing its effect (could be something long term and subtle, who knows) upon them. It's simple and creepy, and I like it.

Also,

Welcome back!

Edit: fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is pretty simple, but sometimes simple is okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is pretty weird, and the picture is definitely creepy. I don't know what to think of it though, it seems a bit too simple (unless I'm missing some glaring implication).

Neutral, but leaning towards up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for reminding me.

item.title: Hello again!
item.content:encoded:
Sorry I dropped off the face of the earth all of a sudden. Things were going badly for me in life and I had to drop all my major time wasting distractions cold turkey to get my life back in order. I'm happy with where I am right now, though, so I plan to ease back in to skip writing.

To kick things off I wanted to tackle Bright's 24 hour challenge. Nothing flashy; just simple weirdness.

Photograph is by Irving Telling, published 1903. Source. Public Domain, verified by Uncle Nicolini.

Discover more of my weird ramblings in
~PeppersGhost's Gallery of Illusion!~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: PUT A RATING MODULE
